frameworks.name: cross-modal interaction
frameworks.one_line: tactile input may attenuate or facilitate visual working-memory processing

frameworks.name: n-back working memory
frameworks.one_line: verbal n-back as visual working-memory load manipulation

core_question: Does concurrent tactile pressure stimulation modulate visual working-memory performance and its neural correlates?
key_assumptions: n-back load taps visual working memory
tactile pressure on the thumbnail is a salient enough cross-modal stimulus to probe interaction
fMRI BOLD reflects task-related cognitive processing
manipulations: presence of tactile pressure stimulation on left thumbnail
key_findings: 2-back yielded higher error rates and longer reaction times than 0-back
task performance was not affected by pressure in either load condition
no significant n-back x pressure interaction in fMRI activity
interpretations: a low-salience tactile stimulus does not perturb visual working-memory behavior or task-related brain activity
limitations_noted: described as a pilot study with small sample
